name: Tail-Call Optimisation
def: Reusing the current stack frame for a function call in tail position, making recursion iterative.
problem: Allow deep/infinite recursion without growing the call stack — essential for recursive FP style.
mechanism: When a call is the last action of a function, the compiler reuses the frame instead of pushing a new one, turning tail recursion into a loop.
limits: Only applies to true tail calls
failures: Assuming TCO where the runtime does not do it → stack overflow
whenNot: Languages/runtimes without TCO (use loops or trampolines)
myths: "All compilers do TCO" — JVM and CPython notably do not guarantee it
